title.note: Title text for the top section of the page.

body2.text: Cedar is a language for defining permissions as policies, and a specification for evaluating those policies. Use Cedar to define who is authorized to do what within your application. Cedar is open source.
body2.note: Paragraph text for the top section of the page.

learnLink.note: A link to the learning path.

body.text: Cedar 4.12 is now released, with public syntax tree (PST) support for variadic-is-in-range and improvements to the experimental type-aware partial evaluation (TPE) feature.
body.note: Announcement text shown in a thin bar below the main section

date.note: A date, shown in bold before the announcement

learnMore.text: To learn more, view the Cedar change log here.

iconAltText.text: An icon of a pair of paintbrushes.
iconAltText.note: Alt text for an icon over the description.

title.note: Title text for a section of the description.

body.text: Cedar is a simple yet expressive language that is purpose-built to support authorization use cases for common authorization models such as RBAC and ABAC.
body.note: Body text for a section of the description.

iconAltText.text: An icon of a gauge display with its needle to the right.
iconAltText.note: Alt text for an icon over the description.

title.note: Title text for a section of the description.

body.text: Cedar is fast and scalable. The policy structure is designed to be indexed for quick retrieval and to support fast and scalable real-time evaluation, with bounded latency.
body.note: Body text for a section of the description.

iconAltText.text: An icon of a magnifying glass.
iconAltText.note: Alt text for an icon over the description.

title.note: Title text for a section of the description.

body.text: Cedar is designed for analysis using Automated Reasoning. This enables analyzer tools capable of optimizing your policies and proving that your security model is what you believe it is.
body.note: Body text for a section of the description.

iconAltText.text: An icon of a lock, representing open source software.
iconAltText.note: Alt text for an icon over the description.

title.note: Title text for a section of the description.

body.text: The Cedar language specification, the Cedar SDK, and much more are available from our GitHub repo, under the terms of the Apache-2.0 license.
body.note: Body text for a section of the description.

title.note: Title text for the get involved section

joinSlack.note: Title text encouraging the user to join us on slack

contribute.note: Title text encouraging the user to contribute to github

title.text: Take control of your security posture
title.note: Title text encouraging the user to use Cedar

unifyCode.text: Stop using slightly different authorization code for every service you deploy, or every single use case.
unifyCode.note: Paragraph text encouraging the user to use Cedar

useCedar.text: With Cedar, you can build it once and deploy it anywhere, with minimal code repetition across services. Offload the access controls to us.
useCedar.note: Paragraph text encouraging the user to use Cedar

title2.note: Title text for a link to integrations.

body2.text: Learn about {avp}, a fully managed AWS service for storing and evaluating Cedar policies. Find out how more companies and services in different verticals are building {integrations} with Cedar today.
body2.note: Paragraph text linking to integrations. Do not translate {integrations}.

title.note: Title text for a link to the reporting page.

body.text: If you discover a potential security issue in this project we ask that you notify us directly via email to cedar-policy-security@lists.cncf.io
body.note: Paragraph text referencing a security contact.

title.note: Text for title of schema tutorial step

label.note: Label text for a tutorial step

paragraph1.text: As soon as your application adds more than a small number of principal and resource entity types, or when you introduce an entity type that has several attributes, you introduce the risk of errors if your policies and your entities don’t agree on the structure or the names of those elements.
paragraph1.note: Part 1 of explanation for schema tutorial step

paragraph2.text: A schema is a formal declaration of the names and structure of your entity types. You declare the name of each type of principal, resource, and action that your application supports. The definition of those entities can also include a list of the attributes that define an entity of that type, with each attribute specifying a name and a data type.
paragraph2.note: Part 2 of explanation for schema tutorial step

paragraph3.text: A schema is defined by using JSON. Schemas have three main properties:
paragraph3.note: Part 3 of explanation for schema tutorial step

namespaceListItem.text: uniquely identifies the schema. The namespace helps you distinguish between elements with the same name from multiple schemas, for example HRApp::File and MedicalRecordsApp::File. The namespace is expressed as the top level key of the schema. Within each namespace, there is a JSON object defining entityTypes, actions, and types.
namespaceListItem.note: Explanation for namespaces in schema tutorial.

entityTypesListItem.text: defines the principal types and resource types supported by your application. Each entity type defines a shape that describes its characteristics. The shape must specify a Cedar supported data type, and define the structure for the more complex types like records or sets. The entity can also specify an optional memberOf attribute to describe how this type can participate in a hierarchy, like files can be in folders, users can be members of groups, or photos can be organized in albums. Note that if you specify that an entity can be a member of its own type, then you are specifying that you can nest them, such as placing folders in other folders.
entityTypesListItem.note: Explanation for entity types in schema tutorial.

actionsListItem.text: defines the operations that the principals can potentially perform on the resources. Each action defines a name for the action, and a list of the resources and principals to which the action can apply.
actionsListItem.note: Explanation for actions in schema tutorial.

typesListItem.text: defines type aliases for complex record types in the schema. Pre-defining types allows for code re-usability whenever the same type is referenced in multiple entities. This key is optional.
typesListItem.note: Explanation for pre-defining types in schema tutorial.

prePolicy1.text: Cedar can validate policies against the schema. This lets you ensure that you are testing the correct structure and use the correct names, including the correct capitalization.
prePolicy1.note: Part 1 explanation for policy in schema tutorial.

prePolicy2.text: If you change any of the actions, or change the principal type from Group or the resource type from Album, then the policy may no longer validate if the entity names no longer match the schema. Give it a try yourself, add entities and play with the policy.
prePolicy2.note: Part 2 explanation for policy in schema tutorial.

primaryActionBtn.note: Text for validate button in schema tutorial.

title.text: Cedarcraft video on Validating Cedar Policies at the Command Line

description.text: This video describes why and how to do schema based validation using the CLI application.

title.text: Cedarcraft video on Cedar VS Code Extension for Easy Policy Validation

description.text: This video describes why and how to do schema based validation using the VS Code extension. See the {blog} for the full set of Cedarcraft videos from {ztatlock}.

title.text: Validating Cedar policies with GitHub Actions

description.text: This blog describes a GitHub Action that can be used by anyone storing Cedar policies in GitHub to validate those policies against a Cedar schema.

label.note: Label text for a tutorial step

title.note: Title text for a tutorial step

paragraph1.text: When you write a complete policy that specifies both the principal and the resource, it’s referred to as a static policy. A static policy is immediately ready for use in authorization decisions.
paragraph1.note: Paragraph text that explains static policies

paragraph2.text: However, sometimes you’ll want to use one set of authorization rules and apply them to many different principals and resources. For example, in a photo sharing application, there might be a common action to share a photo with someone. Performing this action creates a policy for the specified principal and the specified resource. Everything else about the policy is identical in all instances, such as these two samples having the exact same list of actions.
paragraph2.note: Paragraph text that explains a case where static policies become very similar

paragraph3.text: Using static policies in this way can result in a lot of redundant policies that differ only in the principal and resource elements. It also introduces a big issue if you ever need to make a change in the underlying business logic of what sharing a photo means. In that case you’d have to update every one of the individual policies.
paragraph3.note: Paragraph text that explains the issue with static policies used for very similar cases

body.text: Policy templates solve this issue by letting you create policies from a template that uses {placeholders} for the principal or resource, or both. After creating a policy template, you can instantiate individual policies by referencing the template and providing values for the specific principal and resource to use. This template-linked policy then works just like a static policy.
body.note: Paragraph text that explains

paragraph5.text: The placeholders in a template are simply the {principal} and {resource} keywords with a leading {questionMark} character. The following example shows a policy template that implements the same permissions as shown in the previous policy examples. We just don’t yet know what the principals and resources will be.

body.text: Placeholders can appear {only} in the header of a policy, and only on the right-hand side of the {equals} or {in} operators. You can’t use a placeholder for {actions}, and you can’t use a placeholder in a {when} or {unless} clause.
body.note: Paragraph text that explains

paragraph7.text: When you need to create a policy based on that policy template, instead of providing static policy text, you reference the policy template and provide the specific values for each of the placeholders. Cedar then generates a new policy linked to the template with the specified entities.

paragraph8.text: When Cedar instantiates the template, it creates a template-based policy that fills in the placeholders with the values provided by the request to instantiate the policy. For example, an instantiated policy based on the previous template, that specifies {alice} as the principal and {pic} as the resource would behave just as if it had been written as the following static policy.

label.note: Label text for a tutorial step

title.note: Title text for a tutorial step

header.note: Title text for a tutorial step

p1.text: This tutorial provides an introduction to the Cedar policy language. On completion of this tutorial you will be ready to start writing Cedar policies, and understand how those policies will be evaluated for an authorization request.
p1.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step.

p2.text: With Cedar, permissions are expressed as sets of policy statements. Each statement is a rule that permits or forbids a user (or principal) to act on a resource, given a defined context. This format is often referred to as the PARC model.
p2.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step.

header.text: Every policy statement must include an effect and a scope:
header.note: List header text

p1.text: The effect specifies whether this is a {permit} or a {forbid} policy.
p1.note: A bullet point on the list. Do not translate {permit} or {forbid}.

p2.text: The scope specifies the principal[s], the action[s], and the resource[s] to which the effect applies.
p2.note: A bullet point on the list. [s] is meant to show either singular OR plural, like 'principal/principals'.

p3.text: Optionally, the statement may also include one or more conditions in the form of {when} or {unless} clauses.
p3.note: A bullet point on the list. Do not translate {permit} or {forbid}.

p3.text: Through this tutorial we will use a photo sharing application as the inspiration for our examples. Below is an example of a very simple Cedar policy. It has an effect and a scope. The effect is {permit}. The scope is a principal of type {user} called Alice, an action called {update}, and a resource of type {photo} called ‘VacationPhoto94.jpg’. In short, the policy permits a User called Alice to update a Photo called VacationPhoto94.jpg.  Note that this policy has no conditions. We will cover these later in the tutorial.
p3.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step. Do not translate {permit}, {user}, {update}, or {photo.}

p1.text: Principals and resources are identified by a unique combination of a type and an ID, for example, a principal of type ‘User’ called ‘Alice’. For a policy to uniquely reference an entity it must call out both the type and the ID. Alternatively, the {is} operator can be used to reference all entities of a specific type.
p1.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step.

p2.text: Cedar policies are evaluated by a Cedar evaluation engine. The engine considers a set of policy statements in response to an authorization request, and returns either an {allow} or {deny} decision. For the authorization request to be allowed, there must be at least one applicable permit statement and no applicable forbid statements. A policy is applicable if the scope matches that of the authorization request, and all conditions are met.
p2.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step. Do not translate {allow} or {deny}.

p3.text: Within this tutorial, we will present Rust code that illustrates how to use the Cedar library to evaluate authorization requests. This code is not meant to be production quality. The Cedar library is stateless and currently only supports the Rust language. We will provide FFI interfaces into the Cedar library in the future.
p3.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step.

p1.text: Now try editing the policy in the first code block on this page. Change the principal, action, or resource so that the scopes don't match and see that the request denied because the policy no longer applies. Is the evaluation logic case sensitive? Add a second policy underneath the first one, note that you only need one {permit} policy to apply for an {allow} authorization decision.
p1.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step. Do not translate {allow} or {permit}.

p2.text: As an application developer, you can name your entity types (principals and resources) whatever makes sense to you. For these examples, we've used the type name {user}, but we could use {person}, {consumer} or any other label. What matters is that you are consistent between the entity type names you use in your policies and in your code.
p2.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step. Do not translate {user}, {person} or {consumer}.

label.note: Label/title text for a tutorial step. 'Forbid policies' here means 'policies that forbid'.

p1.text: By default, in the absence of an applicable permit policy, the authorization decision will be DENY. Additionally you can create policies that explicitly forbid certain requests. Forbid policies override permit policies. Hence forbid policies can act as guard rails ensuring that certain requests will always be denied regardless of any permit policies.
p1.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step.

p1.text: The policy set expressed above illustrates this point. We see two policies with exactly the same scope, but different effects. The first permits alice to view the photo `VacationPhoto94.jpg`, the second forbids her. When we submit the authorization request below, we expect authorization to be denied.
p1.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step.

p1.text: The sequence in which policies are arranged has no impact on the outcome of the evaluation. Try rearranging the order of the policies above, and confirm that the decision remains the same. For the rest of this tutorial we will use examples of permit policies, but remember that all of the concepts that we introduce using permit policies can also be used with forbid policies.
p1.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step.

label.note: Label text for a tutorial step

p1.text: So far, the example policies we’ve considered have referenced a single principal, action and resource. This would require us to write a separate policy for every permitted combination of these three. Fortunately the Cedar language enables us to write policies with a broader scope. In the case of Actions, we can define a permitted set of actions as part of our policy.
p1.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step.

p1.text: The above policy permits Alice to view, edit and delete the photo called vacationPhoto94.jpg. As you might expect, this evaluates to {allow} for the request below. Within the policy scope you can define sets for actions, but not principals or resources. You can not, for example, define a scope for a set of principals.
p1.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step. Do not translate {allow}.

p1.text: Does the order of items in the set affect the authorization decision? What error does the syntax checker give if you edit the scope to be a set of Users for the principal?
p1.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step.

label.note: Label text for a tutorial step

p1.text: Sets provide one way to broaden the scope of a policy to include more than one action, but they don’t help much for principals and resources, at least within the scope of the policy. There are two ways to write a policy with a broader scope for these entities. The first is to leave the scope completely undefined, by not specifying a principal, resource or action. If a scope is undefined it is considered to apply to all entities.
p1.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step.

p1.text: The policy above leaves the principal undefined and is therefore evaluated as applying to all principals, including Alice. Hence the below request will return an {allow} decision.
p1.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step. Do not translate {allow}.

p1.text: Try editing the policy to leave the resource undefined. What happens if you leave the action undefined?
p1.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step.

p2.text: If we had left the resource undefined, then the policy would allow all principals to perform those three actions on all resources. Make sure to exercise caution when leaving elements of the scope undefined, as this may result in over permissive policies.
p2.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step.

p3.text: Remember that forbid policies can also have undefined scopes. Try adding a second policy that forbids alice to view any resources.
p3.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step.

label.note: Label text for a tutorial step

p1.text: The other way to broaden the scope of a policy is to define it for a group of entities, rather than an individual entity. Principal groups provide us with a way to manage permissions using roles. Let’s imagine users are submitting their photos to a competition for the year’s best vacation photos, and we want a role for the competition judges to be able to view those photos. The policy below permits any principal in the role called {role} to view the photo called {resource}.
p1.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step. Do not translate {role} or {resource}.

p1.text: In this example we are modeling the role as a group. The group is itself an entity and so has a type and an ID. {Role} is not a reserved word, we could equally have called the entity type {UserGroup}. In order to be able to evaluate requests by considering this policy, the evaluation engine needs to know whether the principal referenced within the authorization request is a member of this group. Therefore, your application will need to pass relevant group membership information to the evaluation engine as part of the authorization request. This is done through the {entities} property, which enables you to provide the Cedar evaluation engine with attribute and group membership data for the principal and resource involved in the authorization call. If you look at the code below you will see that group membership is indicated by defining {bob} as having a parent called {bobRole}.
p1.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step. Do not translate {Role}, {UserGroup}, {entities}, {bob}, or {bobRole}.

p1.text: Groups of principals don’t necessarily have to equate to Roles. For example, your application could include a concept of friend groups, whereby permissions on photos are managed based on membership of friend groups. There could be a user group called Jane’s friends, and a policy could be created that gives everyone in that group view access to vacationphoto94.jpg. This policy would be identical in structure to the one in this example.
p1.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step.

p2.text: Note that Bob is actually a member of two groups - {judges} and {juniorJudges}. Try adding a second policy that permits Bob to view vacationPhoto94.jpg through his membership in this second group.
p2.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step. Do not translate {judges} or {juniorJudges}.

p3.text: Policies can also be defined on resource groups. For example, your application might group photos into Albums, and create a policy that applies to all photos in the Album. We provide an example of this at the end of this tutorial.
p3.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step.

label.note: Label text for a tutorial step

header.note: Title text for a tutorial step

p1.text: The policy below permits any principal to view any resource, but only on the condition that the accessLevel for the resource is designated as public. It permits any principal to view any photo, but only on the condition that the resource is tagged as public and the location of the principal is equal to "USA".
p1.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step.

p1.text: Similar to what we saw with groups, the evaluation engine needs to be told the value of the resource attribute called {accessLevel}, in order to be able to evaluate requests by considering this policy. This information needs to be passed by your application, as part of the authorization request using the {entities} request attribute.
p1.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step. Do not translate {accessLevel} or {entities}.

header.text: Experiment by modifying the policy to try out these other operators:
header.note: Paragraph text for the top of a list in a tutorial step.

p1.text: logical or
p1.note: Bullet text for a tutorial step bulletpoint list. 'logical or' refers to 'or' specifically as the boolean operator.

p2.note: Bullet text for a tutorial step bulletpoint list.

header.text: The example above uses an attribute of type {String}. Cedar can also support the following types:
header.note: Paragraph text for the top of a list in a tutorial step. Do not translate {String}.

p1.text: Boolean
p1.note: Bullet text for a tutorial step bulletpoint list.

p2.note: Bullet text for a tutorial step bulletpoint list.

p3.note: Bullet text for a tutorial step bulletpoint list. Do not translate {exampleId}.

p4.text: Sets: collections of values, expressed with []
p4.note: Bullet text for a tutorial step bulletpoint list.

label.note: Label text for a tutorial step.

header.note: Title text for a tutorial step

p1.text: In prior examples our conditions have all been evaluated against static values. Cedar also lets you define conditions that compare the values of different attributes. The example policy below permits any principal to view, edit or delete, any resource, with the condition that the resource owner is the principal.
p1.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step.

p1.text: Note that in this case the resource VacationPhoto94.jpg is described in {entities} as having an attribute of type String called Owner with a value of "alice". The policy compares this attribute to ID of the principal, which is also a string with the value "alice".
p1.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step. Do not translate {entities}.

p1.text: The authorization request in the above example also defines values for the attribute {dept} for both the resource and the principal. Try adding an additional condition to the policy based on this attribute.
p1.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step. Do not translate {dept}.

label.note: Label text for a tutorial step

p1.text: In the examples so far we’ve examined policies based on attributes of the principal and/or the resource. But what if you wanted to write a policy that takes into account variables that are unrelated to either the principal or the resource? Cedar provides a way for you to do this, using the Context record. Context enables you to pass additional data into the authorization request, which can be referenced in policy conditions.
p1.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step.

p2.text: Examples of context data you might use are OpenID claims in a JWT or anything else that is only known at the time of the incoming request and not necessarily stored in your database.
p2.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step.

p3.text: The policy below permits a User called Alice to update and delete a photo called VacationPhoto94.jpg, but only on the condition that she has authenticated using multi-factor authentication and is accessing the application from a specific IP address.
p3.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step.

p1.text: To enable the Cedar evaluation engine to make the authorization decision, we need to supply context values as part of the request. These context values might come from http headers, a signed and verified JWT payload, path parameters, query strings, etc. When using context values, it’s important to remember to structure your policies in such a way that end users cannot spoof the context values. For instance, a good way to ensure a user identity can’t be spoofed is obtaining the user ID from a signed JWT payload.
p1.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step.

p1.text: Experiment with context by changing the policy so that it uses the {oidc_scope} property instead.
p1.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step. Do not translate {oidc_scope}.

p2.text: The {context} and {entities} parameters of the request are different. The {entities} parameter enables the calling application to provide data about the resource and principal, such as attribute values and group membership. Context provides a mechanism for you to write policies that consider other data beyond that of the principal and resource.
p2.note: Paragraph text for a tutorial step. Do not translate {context} or {entities} (appears twice).

cedarVersion.note: Subtitle text to show the version of Cedar in use by WASM, where 'v' is the shortening of 'version'.

description.text: This page has three examples for how Cedar policies can be used to describe and authorize an application’s permissions. The first is a photo-sharing application where Cedar policies determine which users can view particular photos. The second is a Healthcare application where Cedar policies determine who can make, document, and update healthcare appointments, based on their role and attributes. The third is an example authorizing Kubernetes requests.

description.text: How are people using Cedar in production today? Check out the products and services below to find out.

header.note: Header text for the page section

body.text: AWS Verified Access validates each and every application request before granting access. Verified Access removes the need for a VPN, which simplifies the remote connectivity experience for end users and reduces the management complexity for IT administrators.
body.note: Body text for the page section

header.note: Header text for the page section

body2.text: Amazon Verified Permissions is a fully managed service for storing and evaluating Cedar policies. Developers can build applications that use Verified Permissions to authorize each user action.
body2.note: Body text for the page section

header.note: Header text for the Permit.io section

body.text: Permit.io provides access to Cedar as a SaaS service, as well as via OPAL its open-source project. A key enabler for this is Cedar-agent, an open-source project which provides the ability to run Cedar as a standalone agent.
body.note: Body text for the Permit.io section

header.note: Header text for a section

body.text: Using Cedar access controls for Kubernetes, cluster administrators can dynamically create authorization policies that support features like request or user attribute based rules, label-based access controls, conditions, and denial policies.
body.note: Body text for a section

header.note: Header text for a section

body.text: Common Fate provides fast and secure access workflows for cloud infrastructure, data warehouses, identity providers and more. Define Cedar policies in Common Fate to authorize access across multiple cloud providers and integrations.
body.note: Body text for a section

header.note: Header text for a section

body.text: StrongDM is a universal zero trust access platform, providing seamless connectivity, control, and compliance for cloud-native, hybrid, and multi-cloud infrastructure and applications. StrongDM uses Cedar to authorize fine-grained actions across critical infrastructure protocols, including HTTP requests, Kubernetes, interactive shells, and database queries.
body.note: Body text for a section

header.note: Header text for a section

body.text: The Cedarling is an embeddable Policy Decision Point (PDP) that handles logging, JWT validation, and claim mapping. Whether running via the sidecar, WASM or SDK, the Cedarling enables developers to harness the power of the Rust Cedar engine to implement low-latency access control.
body.note: Body text for a section

learnMore.note: Link text for the external learn more link.

description.text: We’ve designed Cedar so that it’s human-readable and easy to learn. Check out the resources below to learn more.

header.note: Header text for the page section

body.text: This tutorial provides an introduction to the Cedar policy language. On completion of this tutorial you will be ready to start writing Cedar policies, and understand how those policies will be evaluated for an authorization request.
body.note: Body text for the page section

button.note: Link text for the external learn more link.

header.note: Header text for the page section

body.text: This guide describes how Cedar uses the schema and policies to provide authorization for your applications. It teaches you how to write policies and create schema using the Cedar policy language.
body.note: Body text for the page section

button.note: Link text for the external learn more link.

estimatedTime.note: Time estimate for learning path item.

description.text: Learn what Cedar is, why it was created, and the basic constructs to be able to create, evaluate and validate policies.

title.text: Watch a short video clip introducing Cedar (from 1:31 to 10:13)

description.text: This video clip introduces Cedar and explains why we built it.
Read the descriptions for the following key terms in the documentation:
Complete the interactive Cedar tutorial to see examples of policies and demonstrate the key constructs of the language.

title.text: Watch Mike Hicks demonstrate the Cedar CLI tool

description.text: Mike demonstrates how to create and evaluate policies using the Cedar CLI tool for the TinyToDo app.
See also this blog from Mike, which walks you through the TinyToDo app that he demos in the above video.

title.text: Watch Fidelity Information Services demonstrate how they use Cedar

description.text: Ana from FIS demonstrates how their Prophet application uses Cedar policies to define and role based permissions that are constrained by attribute based conditions.

title.text: Watch StrongDM demonstrate how they use Cedar

description.text: Justin from StrongDM explains why they chose Cedar and how they use it for data level access.

title.text: Listen to Twilio talk about how they use Cedar

description.text: Hear Peter from Twilio explain why they chose a policy based authorization system over a graph based system.

title.text: Cedar 301 - Anatomy of an authorization request

requestDocs.text: Documentation on constructing an authorization request

entitiesDocs.text: Documentation on entity data and context

description.text: The first section of the workshop will serve as a refresher for some of the content already covered. The sections on 'Entity Sets' and 'Attributes' get into more depth on how to use and pass entity data within your authorization model.

title.text: Watch the Cedarcraft video on Enriching Cedar Policies and the Need for Validation

description.text: This video covers how to handle entity data in Cedar.

title.text: Read guidance on Cedar policy design patterns

description.text: Read the design patterns section in the Cedar documentation to learn how Cedar can be used to implement Role Based Access Control (RBAC) and Relationship based Access Control (ReBAC).

title.text: Cedar 303 - Learn why AWS created Cedar, and how we went about it

description.text: Emina, one of the originators of Cedar, explains why and how AWS set about creating Cedar.

title.text: How we built Cedar with automated reasoning and differential testing

description.text: Mike, another Cedar creator, explains how we use automated reasoning to prove important correctness properties about formal models of Cedar's components, and then differential random testing to show that the models match the production code.
Read the blog explaining why a core tenet of Cedar is its analyzability.
A report from security advisors Trail of Bits providing a comparison of the Cedar, Rego and OpenFGA languages.

title.text: Cedar: A New Language for Expressive, Fast, Safe, and Analyzable Authorization

description.text: The article compares Cedar to two open-source languages, OpenFGA and Rego, and finds (subjectively) that Cedar has equally or more readable policies, but (objectively) performs far better.

title.text: A Quick Guide To Partial Evaluation

description.text: Read the blog explaining how to use the experimental feature Partial Evaluation to construct data queries. Note that this is currently an experimental feature and should not be used in production.
Visit the Cedar GitHub repo, download the SDK and start building. Review and comment on RFCs and submit pull requests.

namespace.note: Title for namespace explanantion in schema tutorial.

schema.note: Title for Schema in schema tutorial.

cedar.note: Title for Cedar in schema tutorial.

header.note: Page header text

openSource.text: Note: The Cedar SDK is open source, which means you don't need to use Amazon Verified Permissions. Think of Amazon Verified Permissions as a tool to help you work with the Cedar SDK effectively.

serviceInfo.text: Amazon Verified Permissions is a fully managed service for storing and evaluating Cedar policies. Developers can build applications that use Verified Permissions to authorize each user action.

title.text: Why would I use Amazon Verified Permissions?

secureStorage.text: A secure place to store and manage Cedar schemas and policies.

console.text: Console wizards that help you build your Cedar schema and connect to different Identity Providers.

protectedApis.text: Protected APIs, so that only authorized parties can access and change policies.

resilientService.text: A resilient authorization service that scales to thousands of requests per second across multiple regions.

authzInCloudtrail.text: All authorization requests are logged in AWS CloudTrail, providing a complete record of who accessed what.

managementInCloudtrail.text: All management operations are logged in AWS CloudTrail, so you know who granted access and when.

body.text: Amazon Verified Permissions is an AWS service, which means you need an AWS account to use it. If you don't have an account, you can sign up {here}, and try out Verified Permissions.
body.note: Paragraph text

body.text: Once you have an account set up, {followSteps}.
body.note: Paragraph text

followSteps.text: follow these steps to create your first policy store

header.text: Getting started with Amazon Verified Permissions
header.note: Page header text

body.text: Sign in to the AWS console and navigate to the {avpPage}.
body.note: Paragraph text

startWizard.text: Click 'Get started with Verified Permissions', and then click 'Create policy store'.

makeSamplePStore.text: Select 'Start from a sample policy store', choose ''{PhotoFlash}'', and click 'Create policy store'. Once creation finishes, click 'Go to overview' at the top of the screen.

navToCreatePolicy.text: Select 'Policies' from the sidebar menu on the left. Click on 'Create policy', and choose 'Create static policy'.

finishPolicyCreation.text: Click 'Next' at the bottom of the page. Add a policy description (optional) and click 'Create Policy'.

navToTestBench.text: Select 'Test bench' from the sidebar menu on the left.

runAuthorization.text: At the top of the page, click 'Run authorization request'.

expectedResult.text: Based on the policy you created, Verified Permissions should evaluate this request and return a decision of 'Allow' in a message bar at the top of the page.

avp.note: Link text of the name of the AVP service.
